title: Area of Composite Figures
why: A composite figure is just simpler shapes stuck together. You find each piece's area and add them up. So the feeder skills are rectangle and triangle area plus adding areas. Warm those up and any L-shape or house-shape becomes a few easy pieces.

diagnostic.q: A rectangle is 4 by 3. What is its area?
A triangle has base 4 and height 3. Its area is base × height ÷ 2 = ?

learn.heading: Break it apart, then add
learn.intro: A composite figure is made of simple shapes (rectangles and triangles) joined together. The trick: break it into pieces, find each piece's area, then add the areas together.
examples: L-shape: split into two rectangles, say 12 and 8. Total area = 12 + 8 = 20 square units.
House shape: a rectangle (area 20) plus a triangle roof (area 6). Total = 20 + 6 = 26 square units.

items.q: A. Rectangle one is 3 by 4. Area = 3 × 4 = ___?
B. Rectangle two is 2 by 4. Area = 2 × 4 = ___?

tiers.intro: Add a rectangle and a triangle.

items.q: A. A rectangle is 5 by 4. Area = ___?
B. A triangle has base 4 and height 3. Area = base × height ÷ 2 = ___?
A. Rectangle 6 by 5, plus rectangle 2 by 5. Find both, then add: ___?
B. A figure has a rectangle of area 24 and a triangle of area 9. Total area = ___?

exit.q: 1. Two pieces have areas 18 and 7. What is the total area?
2. A rectangle of area 30 and a triangle of area 5 are joined. Total area = ___?
